chapter: Chapter: Weavers, Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

questions.question: Why were Indian textiles famous in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries?
questions.answer: Indian textiles were famous for their fine quality, beautiful designs and bright colours, which made them popular in international markets.

questions.question: What role did merchants play in the Indian textile trade?
questions.answer: Merchants supplied raw materials to weavers and sold the finished cloth in markets or exported it to other countries.

questions.question: Why did the East India Company want control over Indian textile production?
questions.answer: The Company wanted control to ensure a steady supply of textiles and earn large profits from trade.

questions.question: Who were the gomasthas and what was their role?
questions.answer: Gomasthas were agents of the East India Company who supervised weavers and ensured that they supplied cloth to the Company.

questions.question: Why did weavers often accept advances from the East India Company?
questions.answer: Weavers accepted advances because they needed money to buy raw materials and support their families.

questions.question: What difficulties did weavers face under the control of the East India Company?
questions.answer: Weavers were forced to sell cloth only to the Company at low prices and were not free to sell to other buyers.

questions.question: Why did many weavers become poor during British rule?
questions.answer: They became poor because machine-made cloth from Britain reduced demand for handwoven cloth.

questions.question: What technological change increased textile production in Britain?
questions.answer: The introduction of machines such as the power loom increased textile production in Britain.

questions.question: Why was machine-made cloth cheaper than handwoven cloth?
questions.answer: Machine-made cloth was produced faster and in large quantities, which reduced production costs.

questions.question: How did the Industrial Revolution affect Indian weavers?
questions.answer: The Industrial Revolution led to the import of machine-made cloth from Britain, which reduced demand for Indian handwoven textiles.

questions.question: Why did the demand for Indian textiles decline in the nineteenth century?
questions.answer: Demand declined because British factories produced cheaper machine-made textiles.

questions.question: What role did factories play in the textile industry?
questions.answer: Factories produced textiles using machines and workers, which increased production.

questions.question: Why did Indian entrepreneurs start textile mills?
questions.answer: Indian entrepreneurs started mills to compete with imported British cloth and meet the growing demand for textiles.

questions.question: Name one city where textile mills developed in India.
questions.answer: Bombay became an important centre for textile mills in India.

questions.question: What was the main raw material used in textile factories?
questions.answer: Cotton was the main raw material used in textile factories.

questions.question: Why did factory industries grow in the late nineteenth century?
questions.answer: Factory industries grew due to new technology, increasing demand and availability of raw materials.

questions.question: How did factory production differ from handloom weaving?
questions.answer: Factory production used machines and produced goods faster, while handloom weaving was slower and done manually.

questions.question: Why did many traditional industries decline during colonial rule?
questions.answer: Traditional industries declined because they could not compete with cheaper machine-made goods.

questions.question: What role did factory owners play in industrial development?
questions.answer: Factory owners invested money, organized production and managed industries.

questions.question: How did railways help the growth of textile mills?
questions.answer: Railways helped transport raw materials and finished goods quickly.

questions.question: Why did factory-made goods dominate markets?
questions.answer: Factory-made goods were cheaper and produced in large quantities.

questions.question: What was the effect of British industrialization on Indian industries?
questions.answer: British industrialization led to the decline of traditional Indian industries such as handloom weaving.

questions.question: Why were Indian handloom weavers unable to compete with British factories?
questions.answer: They could not match the speed and low cost of machine production.

questions.question: What change occurred in textile production after factories were established?
questions.answer: Production shifted from small handloom units to large machine-based factories.

questions.question: How did industrialization change the structure of production in India?
questions.answer: Industrialization introduced machine-based production and large-scale factory industries.
